region_of_origin: China (widely adopted across Southeast Asia including Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 covers dumpling food products broadly; no specific fried handmade dumpling subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EKL covers filled wheat-based dumplings; fried preparation variant is the closest defensible match within the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for fried handmade dumplings. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food preparation codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried handmade dumplings is 6–8 pieces (approximately 150 g), commonly served as a snack or appetiser in Singapore hawker centres and Asian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Dumplings, meat-filled, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input nutrient values (energy 233 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 13.3 g, carbohydrate 18.7 g, sodium 433 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for fried meat-filled dumplings (FDC ID 168927 and related entries). Values cross-referenced against Singapore HPB Foodo database for fried dumplings. Input values retained as they fall within the plausible range for this food type. Source match type is 'adapted' because no single USDA entry is an exact match for handmade Singaporean-style fried dumplings; the closest USDA fried dumpling entry was used for cross-validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried handmade dumplings in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food: wheat-flour-based filled dumplings / dim sum items with mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate composition. Wheat-based dim sum items with meat filling typically fall in the GI range 48–60 per published analogous data. GI 55 used as a central estimate; this value falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). gi_category is set to 'low' consistent with the University of Sydney GI classification threshold. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 28.0 g) / 100 = 15.4 g (150 g serving × 18.67 g carbohydrate/100 g = 28.0 g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg per 100 g; estimated 650 mg per standard 150 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fried dumpling intake to no more than 3–4 pieces per occasion, request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the filling, and avoid additional dipping sauces, as a single 150 g serving provides approximately 33% of the 2000 mg daily sodium target recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour wrapper with GI ~55 and 18.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g; fat from frying may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 3–4 pieces (approximately 75 g) to keep glycaemic load below 8 g, choose steamed dumplings over fried to reduce fat-mediated insulin resistance, and avoid sweet dipping sauces that add further rapidly absorbed carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.33 g per 100 g, trans fat 0.07 g per 100 g, cholesterol 26.67 mg per 100 g, and high sodium from soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit fried dumpling consumption due to combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium load; steamed preparation is preferred, and portion should be restricted to 3–4 pieces to keep saturated fat intake below 2 g per snack occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (pork) provides arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways; pan-frying or deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium exceeds 400 mg per 100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid fried pork dumplings due to the combined pro-inflammatory effect of arachidonic acid from minced pork, AGE formation from frying, and high sodium; steamed chicken or tofu-filled dumplings are a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Handmade Dumplings (手工煎饺, Shǒugōng Jiān Jiǎo) are a popular Asian snack and appetiser consisting of thin wheat-flour wrappers hand-folded around a savoury filling of minced pork, cabbage, chives, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil, then pan-fried or deep-fried until the exterior is golden-brown and crispy while the interior remains juicy and tender. The dish originates from Chinese culinary tradition and is widely consumed across Singapore, China, and Southeast Asia. Per 100 g, fried handmade dumplings provide approximately 233 kcal, 10 g protein, 13.3 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 18.7 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 433 mg sodium. The high sodium content from soy sauce seasoning makes this food a concern for individuals with hypertension. The refined wheat flour wrapper contributes a low-to-moderate glycaemic index (~55) and a glycaemic load of approximately 15 g per standard 150 g serving, requiring portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The combination of saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which is a consideration for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. A standard serving is 6–8 pieces (approximately 150 g). Commonly served with chilli sauce, black vinegar, or soy-ginger dipping sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for fried meat-filled dumplings and cross-referenced against Singapore HPB Foodo database ranges. No single approved source provides an exact match for handmade Singaporean-style fried dumplings; values are adapted from the closest USDA fried dumpling entry. GI is an analogous estimate from published wheat-based dim sum data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for handmade fried dumplings of this specific Singaporean preparation style are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not provide a granular micronutrient breakdown for this specific item. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 13.33, carbohydrate_g 18.67) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a fried meat-and-vegetable filled wheat dumpling; no zero-macro justification is required. Cultural tags in the input ('Ramen', 'Pizza', 'Pasta') were removed as they are not applicable to this dish. Country of origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'China (widely adopted across Southeast Asia including Singapore)' to reflect the dish's culinary origin. Halal status set to false as default because pork is a primary filling ingredient; this dish is not halal. gi_category set to 'low' consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification threshold where GI ≤55 is classified as low; GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low range. ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 entries, as no exact subclass exists for fried handmade dumplings in either system.
